questions.questionText: Atropine is a muscarinic receptor antagonist that causes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Atropine: competitive muscarinic (M) antagonist → blocks parasympathetic effects. Effects: mydriasis (pupil dilation, NOT miosis), cycloplegia, tachycardia (blocks vagal tone), dry mouth (decreased salivary secretion — most sensitive), decreased GI motility, urinary retention, decreased sweating, bronchodilation. Uses: pre-anesthetic (reduce secretions), bradycardia, organophosphate poisoning (muscarinic symptoms), mydriatic. Contraindicated: glaucoma, BPH. Reference: KD Tripathi — Anticholinergics.

questions.optionB: Atropine (muscarinic blockade) + pralidoxime (PAM — reactivates AChE before 'aging')
questions.explanation: Organophosphates (malathion, parathion, nerve gases): irreversible AChE inhibitors → excess ACh → cholinergic crisis (DUMBBBELS: Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia, Bronchospasm/Bronchorrhea, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation). Treatment: (1) Atropine (blocks muscarinic effects — first-line), (2) Pralidoxime/2-PAM (reactivates AChE by cleaving OP-enzyme bond — give within 24-48h BEFORE 'aging'). PAM does NOT cross BBB. Decontamination. Reference: KD Tripathi — OP Poisoning.

questions.questionText: Propranolol is a non-selective β-blocker that is contraindicated in:
questions.explanation: Propranolol: non-selective β-blocker (blocks β1 and β2). β2 blockade → bronchospasm → CONTRAINDICATED in asthma/COPD. β1 effects: decreased HR, decreased contractility, decreased AV conduction, decreased renin release. Uses: hypertension, angina, arrhythmias, thyrotoxicosis (symptoms), migraine prophylaxis, anxiety (performance), essential tremor. Cardioselective β1-blockers (metoprolol, atenolol, bisoprolol): safer in mild asthma (still use with caution). Reference: KD Tripathi — Beta-Blockers.

questions.optionB: All adrenergic receptors: α1, α2, β1, β2 (nonselective agonist at physiological doses)
questions.explanation: Epinephrine: nonselective adrenergic agonist (α1, α2, β1, β2, β3). At low doses: β effects predominate (vasodilation β2, bronchodilation β2, increased HR β1). At high doses: α effects (vasoconstriction α1) predominate. Uses: anaphylaxis (drug of choice — IM), cardiac arrest, severe asthma, local anesthetic adjunct (reduces bleeding, prolongs action). Epinephrine reversal: α-blockers (phentolamine) → unmasked β2 → vasodilation and hypotension. Reference: KD Tripathi — Epinephrine.

questions.optionB: Myasthenia gravis (reversible AChE inhibitor — does NOT cross BBB), post-operative urinary retention, reversal of neuromuscular blockade
questions.explanation: Neostigmine: quaternary ammonium (charged) → does NOT cross BBB. Reversible AChE inhibitor → increases ACh at NMJ and muscarinic sites. Uses: (1) Myasthenia gravis (mainstay treatment for symptomatic relief), (2) Reversal of non-depolarizing NMJ blockade (post-surgery — given with atropine/glycopyrrolate to block muscarinic side effects), (3) Post-operative urinary retention, paralytic ileus. Pyridostigmine: longer-acting, preferred for MG maintenance. Physostigmine: crosses BBB (tertiary amine). Reference: KD Tripathi — Anticholinesterases.

questions.questionText: Prazosin is an α1-selective antagonist used for:
questions.explanation: Prazosin: selective α1-blocker → relaxes vascular smooth muscle (vasodilation → decreases BP) and prostatic smooth muscle (relieves urinary obstruction in BPH). Side effect: first-dose orthostatic hypotension (give at bedtime, start low). Other α1-blockers for BPH: tamsulosin (uroselective — less hypotension), terasin, doxazosin. Phentolamine: non-selective α-blocker (α1 + α2) — used for pheochromocytoma diagnosis/crisis. Phenoxybenzamine: irreversible α-blocker (pre-op pheochromocytoma). Reference: KD Tripathi — α-Blockers.

questions.questionText: Succinylcholine is a depolarizing neuromuscular blocker that can cause:
questions.optionB: Hyperkalemia, malignant hyperthermia (in susceptible individuals), muscle fasciculations, and prolonged apnea (in pseudocholinesterase deficiency)
questions.explanation: Succinylcholine (suxamethonium): depolarizing NMJ blocker (Phase I — initial fasciculations then paralysis). Ultra-short acting (metabolized by plasma pseudocholinesterase/butyrylcholinesterase). Complications: hyperkalemia (dangerous in burns, crush injuries, spinal cord injury, renal failure — upregulated extrajunctional nicotinic receptors), malignant hyperthermia (with volatile anesthetics, RYR1 mutation — treat with dantrolene), prolonged apnea (pseudocholinesterase deficiency). Reference: KD Tripathi — NMJ Blockers.

questions.questionText: Pilocarpine is used in glaucoma because it:
questions.optionB: Causes miosis (contracts pupillary sphincter) → opens trabecular meshwork → increases aqueous humor outflow
questions.explanation: Pilocarpine: direct-acting muscarinic (M3) agonist → contracts pupillary sphincter (miosis) and ciliary muscle → pulls on scleral spur → opens trabecular meshwork → increases outflow of aqueous humor → lowers IOP. Used for: open-angle glaucoma, acute angle-closure glaucoma (emergency — miosis pulls iris away from angle). Side effects: accommodative spasm (near vision blur), brow ache, miosis. Other glaucoma drugs: timolol (β-blocker, decreases production), latanoprost (PGF2α analog, increases uveoscleral outflow). Reference: KD Tripathi — Glaucoma.
